description: A digital Table 6.5 recorder for NCERT Grade 6 Science, Chapter 6 — Materials Around Us, Activity 6.7. Five fixed material rows (Sugar, Salt, Chalk powder, Sand, Sawdust). Three columns: Predict (student chooses 'Dissolves' / 'Does not'), Observe (same toggle after the experiment), Match (auto-filled Yes/No by comparing predict vs observe). A dynamic summary bar shows 'X of 5 predictions matched'. A synthesis text field prompts a one-sentence pattern statement; on submit it reveals the textbook explanation plus four key-concept pills. Wrong observation entries show a contextual hint. Confetti on synthesis submission. Fully responsive — compact pill toggles on mobile.

chapter.subject: Science
chapter.section: 6.3.4 What is soluble in water; what is not?
chapter.table: Table 6.5 — Mixing different materials in water

width.description: Maximum container width in pixels. Fully responsive below this.

themeColor.description: Primary accent colour used in header gradient and primary buttons.

darkMode.description: Reserved for a future dark theme.

additionalProps.description: Tool-specific configuration. All fields are optional — Activity 6.7 defaults are used.

materials.description: Override the five default material rows. Each item is one row in the table.

name.description: Display name shown in the Material column.

emoji.description: Single emoji for the material icon.

correctPredict.description: The scientifically correct answer for the Predict column.

correctObserve.description: The scientifically correct answer for the Observe column (usually same as correctPredict).

hintText.description: Short hint shown under the row when the student's observation is wrong.

examples.name: Glucose
examples.hintText: Glucose is a sugar — it dissolves fully and makes water taste sweet.

showCorrectness.description: When true, wrong Observe entries show a ⚠️ 'Check again' label under the toggle.

showHints.description: When true, a contextual hint banner is shown below any row with a wrong Observe entry.

synthesisPrompt.default: Looking at your table, which kinds of solids dissolve in water and which do not? Write one sentence describing the pattern.
synthesisPrompt.description: Custom question shown above the synthesis text field.

successMessage.default: Great observations! You matched the textbook results.
successMessage.description: Message shown in the summary bar when all rows are complete but not all predictions matched.

examples.description: Default — exact Activity 6.7 with five NCERT materials
Extended set — add glucose and wheat flour

materials.name: Sugar
materials.hintText: Sugar breaks into invisible particles — the solution is clear and sweet.

materials.name: Salt
materials.hintText: Salt is highly soluble — it disappears quickly when stirred in water.

materials.name: Chalk powder
materials.hintText: Chalk forms a cloudy suspension that settles over time.

materials.name: Sand
materials.hintText: Sand is insoluble — it sinks even after vigorous stirring.

materials.name: Sawdust
materials.hintText: Sawdust floats or remains visible after stirring.

materials.name: Glucose
materials.hintText: Glucose is a sugar — it dissolves fully and makes water taste sweet.

materials.name: Wheat flour
materials.hintText: Flour does not dissolve — it forms a cloudy mixture and eventually settles.

value.synthesisPrompt: Based on your extended table, write one rule about which kinds of household solids dissolve in water.
value.successMessage: Excellent! Your extended observations are accurate.

value.synthesisPrompt: Write one sentence: what pattern do you see in your results?

defaultMaterials.name: Sugar
defaultMaterials.hintText: Sugar breaks into invisible particles — the solution is clear and sweet.

defaultMaterials.name: Salt
defaultMaterials.hintText: Salt is highly soluble — it disappears quickly when stirred in water.

defaultMaterials.name: Chalk powder
defaultMaterials.hintText: Chalk forms a cloudy suspension that settles over time.

defaultMaterials.name: Sand
defaultMaterials.hintText: Sand is insoluble — it sinks even after vigorous stirring.

defaultMaterials.name: Sawdust
defaultMaterials.hintText: Sawdust floats or remains visible after stirring.

predict.description: Student's prediction before stirring. Two-button pill toggle: Dissolves / Does not.

observe.description: Student's actual observation after stirring. Same two-button toggle.

match.description: Auto-filled. Green ✓ if predict === observe, red ✗ if different. Blank if either column is empty.

summaryBar.description: Dynamic bar below the table. Shows 'X of Y rows completed' while in progress, 'X of Y predictions matched observations' when all filled. Mini match-pill badges per material. Turns green when all rows are complete.

synthesisSection.description: Text area prompting a one-sentence pattern statement. Submit button enabled when input ≥ 6 characters. On submit: shows student's answer in a green card, textbook explanation in a purple card, and four key-concept pills (Soluble, Insoluble, Dissolved ≠ destroyed, Physical change).
synthesisSection.textbookPattern: Soluble materials (sugar, salt) completely disappear when stirred — their particles are too small to see but are still present. Insoluble materials (chalk powder, sand, sawdust) do not mix with water and remain visible even after stirring.

interactions.verdictToggle: Pill-style two-button toggle. Tap once to select; tap again to deselect (returns to empty state). Green for 'Dissolves', red for 'Does not'.
interactions.matchBadge: Auto-renders as grey dash (–) until both predict and observe are filled. Then green ✓ or red ✗ with a pop-in animation.
interactions.wrongObserve: Shows ⚠️ 'Check again' label below the Observe toggle. If showHints is true, an amber hint banner appears below the row.
interactions.synthesisSubmit: Disabled (greyed) until synthesis.trim().length ≥ 6. On submit: green answer card + purple textbook card + key concept pills + confetti. 'Edit answer' button returns to input. 'Start over' resets entire tool.
interactions.reset: Clears all toggle selections, synthesis text, and submitted state.

responsive.mobile: Compact pill labels ('✓ Yes' / '✗ No'), smaller emoji icons, stacked summary bar
responsive.tabletPlus: Full 'Dissolves' / 'Does not' labels, 36px emoji icons, horizontal summary bar

agentNotes.whenToUse: Use when a Grade 6 student is doing or has done Activity 6.7 (mixing materials in water) from section 6.3.4 of the Curiosity textbook. Also use as a standalone data-recording activity when teaching solubility concepts.
coreConceptsReinforced: Prediction vs observation as distinct scientific skills
Dissolving is a physical change — matter is conserved
Scientific synthesis — drawing conclusions from observations
agentNotes.instructionLine: Write your predictions and observations for each material. Then describe the pattern you see.
